project_to_default_route.single_storey_extension: Full plans is the safest default if foundations, drainage, steelwork, large glazing or open-plan changes are involved. A building notice may be acceptable for simple domestic work, but it gives less design certainty before work starts.
project_to_default_route.two_storey_extension: Use full plans unless a professional confirms a simpler route is suitable. Two-storey work increases structural, fire, drainage and neighbour risk.
project_to_default_route.loft_conversion: Full plans should be the default because structure, stairs, fire safety and insulation need design before work starts.
project_to_default_route.garage_conversion: Usually needs building regulations approval. Full plans is better where the garage floor, wall insulation, damp proofing and fire separation need checking.
project_to_default_route.removing_load_bearing_wall: Treat as structural work. Use full plans or a clear building control route with structural calculations before work starts.
project_to_default_route.chimney_breast_removal: Use structural design and building control approval before removal. Party wall and fireplace/flue issues may be separate.
project_to_default_route.bathroom: Minor like-for-like changes may not need an application, but new drainage, electrical work, ventilation and structural changes can trigger approval/certification.
project_to_default_route.kitchen: Simple replacement may be low-risk, but moving drainage, gas, electrics, extract, walls or windows changes the compliance picture.
project_to_default_route.windows_doors: Often handled through a competent person scheme for registered installers. Keep the compliance certificate.
project_to_default_route.boiler_heating: Use a registered competent person where possible. Keep Gas Safe/OFTEC/HETAS or other relevant certificates and commissioning records.
project_to_default_route.electrical_work: Notifiable domestic electrical work needs either building control involvement or a registered installer who can self-certify.
project_to_default_route.solar_panels: Building regulations can cover structure, electrics and fire/equipment safety even where planning is permitted development.
project_to_default_route.heat_pump: Use competent installers and keep commissioning, electrical and product records. Planning/noise constraints may still apply.
project_to_default_route.garden_room: Detached small buildings can be exempt only in limited circumstances. Sleep accommodation, services, size, boundary proximity and construction change the route.
project_to_default_route.outbuilding: Check exemption rules early; do not assume every shed, office or gym is exempt.
project_to_default_route.basement: Use full plans and specialist design. Basements are high-risk because structure, water, escape, ventilation and drainage interact.
project_to_default_route.underpinning: Use full plans and engineer-led design. Local authority/approver inspection timing matters because work is concealed quickly.
project_to_default_route.rooflights: Small rooflights may be simple, but structure, insulation, safety glass, ventilation and fire escape roles must be checked.
project_to_default_route.roof_alterations: Treat structural roof changes as building control work unless clearly minor and non-structural.
project_to_default_route.driveway_drainage: Planning and drainage rules often overlap. Building regulations are mainly relevant where drainage connects, diverts or affects building fabric.
project_to_default_route.insulation: Thermal upgrades can trigger Part L and can create moisture/ventilation/fire risks if treated as decoration only.
project_to_default_route.porch: Some small porches may be exempt if conditions are met, but glazing, electrics, thermal separation and structure still matter.
project_to_default_route.flat_alterations: Work to flats is higher sensitivity. Check lease/freeholder, fire strategy, sound, structure and building control before starting.
project_to_default_route.new_dwelling_conversion: Use full plans and professional design. Creating a new dwelling pulls in the widest set of Approved Documents and usually has planning/change-of-use overlap.

P: Building Regulations compliance certificate or notice
7: CE/UKCA or equivalent evidence where applicable
photos of installed products before cover-up

A: work near party walls or shared structures
B: open-plan ground floor below upper-storey bedrooms
removing doors that protected escape routes
C: floor levels close to external ground
G: high water-use fittings in new dwellings
H: building over or near a public sewer
surface water discharged to combined sewer unnecessarily
L: using old Part L assumptions after 2026 updates
O: restricted window opening due to noise/security
roof rooms with high solar gain
Q: cheap replacement units with no security documentation
R: omitting telecoms infrastructure until late build
T: assuming domestic-only rules apply to non-domestic use

global_flags.message: This site is England-first. Wales, Scotland and Northern Ireland have separate building standards routes.
Higher-risk buildings need Building Safety Regulator routing rather than ordinary domestic building-control routing.
Completed work without evidence may require regularisation or certificate tracing.
Building regulations and planning permission are separate; check the planning side before starting.
